# Introduction

Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is the most common subtype of non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), accounting for approximately 30--40% of all new NHL diagnoses worldwide [@Li2018Diffuse; @SusanibarAdaniya2021]. With an estimated annual incidence of 5--7 cases per 100,000 persons in Western countries, DLBCL represents a substantial clinical and public health burden that spans all adult age groups, though incidence increases with advancing age [@Lewis2020Lymphoma]. The disease is characterized by aggressive clinical behavior, rapid proliferation of large transformed B lymphocytes, and marked biological heterogeneity that confounds uniform treatment approaches [@Li2018Diffuse]. Left untreated, DLBCL follows a rapidly fatal course; however, the introduction of curative-intent chemoimmunotherapy has fundamentally altered its natural history, transforming it from a uniformly lethal disease into one in which long-term remission is achievable for a majority of patients.

The modern era of DLBCL treatment began with the landmark demonstration that the addition of rituximab to cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (CHOP) substantially improved overall survival [@Coiffier2016Diffuse]. The R-CHOP regimen has since served as the undisputed standard of care for over two decades, curing approximately 60% of patients with newly diagnosed DLBCL [@Bartlett2019Doseadjusted; @SusanibarAdaniya2021]. Patients who achieve event-free survival at 24 months experience minimal excess mortality compared with age-matched populations, underscoring the curative potential of frontline therapy when it succeeds [@Jakobsen2017Minimal]. However, for the approximately 40% of patients who experience primary refractory disease or relapse following R-CHOP, outcomes remain dismal. The international SCHOLAR-1 study demonstrated that patients with refractory DLBCL have an objective response rate of only 26% to subsequent therapy and a median overall survival of 6.3 months, highlighting the critical importance of optimizing frontline treatment [@Crump2017Outcomes].

Despite the enduring success of R-CHOP, numerous efforts to improve upon this regimen over the past two decades met with repeated failure, underscoring both the robustness of the standard and the difficulty of surpassing it. Dose-adjusted EPOCH-R did not demonstrate superiority over R-CHOP in the phase III CALGB 50303 trial [@Bartlett2019Doseadjusted]. Substitution of obinutuzumab for rituximab in the GOYA trial failed to improve progression-free or overall survival [@Vitolo2017Obinutuzumab]. Intensification strategies incorporating high-dose chemotherapy and autologous stem-cell transplantation as frontline consolidation have shown inconsistent benefits, even in high-risk populations [@Chiappella2017Rituximabdosedense]. These failures established R-CHOP as a remarkably resilient standard, resistant to displacement by conventional chemotherapy modifications alone. Notably, even in the FLYER trial, which demonstrated that four cycles of CHOP were non-inferior to six cycles in young patients with favorable-risk disease, rituximab remained the indispensable backbone component [@Poeschel2019Four].

The biological heterogeneity of DLBCL has emerged as a central explanation for the limitations of uniform treatment approaches. Cell-of-origin (COO) classification, initially defined by gene expression profiling, distinguishes germinal center B-cell--like (GCB) and activated B-cell--like (ABC) subtypes that arise through distinct oncogenic pathways and exhibit different clinical behaviors [@Lacy2020Targeted; @Staiger2017Clinical]. The ABC subtype, characterized by constitutive activation of the NF-kB pathway and B-cell receptor signaling, has historically been associated with inferior outcomes following R-CHOP [@Ennishi2019Doublehit]. Simultaneously, the identification of double-hit lymphoma---defined by concurrent rearrangements of MYC and BCL2 or BCL6---delineated a particularly aggressive subgroup with poor prognosis under standard therapy [@Sha2019Molecular; @Landsburg2016Sole]. Double-expresser lymphoma, defined by concurrent protein overexpression of MYC and BCL2 without underlying genetic rearrangements, represents an additional high-risk category [@Staiger2017Clinical]. More recently, comprehensive genomic classification systems have defined molecular subtypes based on recurrent patterns of genetic alterations, promising a more refined framework for precision therapy [@Lacy2020Targeted; @Rossi2017Diffuse].

These molecular insights generated considerable enthusiasm for subtype-directed frontline therapy. The phase III PHOENIX trial evaluated the addition of ibrutinib, a Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor targeting the B-cell receptor signaling pathway, to R-CHOP in non-GCB DLBCL [@Younes2019Randomized; @Pal2018Role]. Although a prespecified subgroup of younger patients with ABC-type DLBCL showed a trend toward benefit, the overall trial did not meet its primary endpoint, and excess toxicity in older patients dampened enthusiasm [@Wilson2021Effect]. Similarly, the ROBUST trial combining lenalidomide with R-CHOP in ABC-type DLBCL failed to demonstrate a progression-free survival advantage, further challenging the hypothesis that COO-directed therapy alone could overcome treatment resistance [@Nowakowski2021ROBUST]. The REMoDL-B trial testing bortezomib plus R-CHOP likewise failed to improve outcomes in ABC-type disease despite strong biological rationale [@Davies2019Geneexpression]. Collectively, these negative results illustrated that molecular subtype alone is an insufficient basis for treatment selection and that the underlying biology of treatment resistance is more complex than initially appreciated.

Against this backdrop, the antibody--drug conjugate (ADC) polatuzumab vedotin emerged as the first agent to demonstrably improve upon R-CHOP in a randomized phase III trial. Polatuzumab vedotin, which delivers the microtubule-disrupting agent monomethyl auristatin E to CD79b-expressing B cells, showed promising activity in the relapsed and refractory setting [@Sehn2020Polatuzumab; @Morschhauser2019Polatuzumab; @Deeks2019Polatuzumab]. The subsequent POLARIX trial demonstrated that polatuzumab vedotin plus rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and prednisone (pola-R-CHP) significantly improved progression-free survival compared with R-CHOP in previously untreated DLBCL, representing the first successful modification of the R-CHOP backbone in two decades [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab]. This achievement validated the ADC platform as a viable strategy for frontline therapy and has begun to reshape clinical practice [@Chau2019Antibodydrug; @Joubert2020Antibodydrug].

Concurrently, the chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy revolution has transformed the management of relapsed and refractory large B-cell lymphoma, with three CD19-directed CAR-T products demonstrating durable remissions in heavily pretreated patients [@Neelapu2023Fiveyear; @Bethge2022GLADRST]. The ZUMA-7 and TRANSFORM trials established CAR-T therapy as superior to standard salvage chemotherapy and autologous transplantation in the second-line setting [@Kamdar2022Lisocabtagene; @Bishop2022SecondLine], while the PILOT study demonstrated feasibility in transplant-ineligible patients [@Sehgal2022Lisocabtagene]. The ZUMA-12 trial has provided preliminary evidence supporting CAR-T consolidation in high-risk frontline patients [@Neelapu2022Axicabtagene], and bispecific antibodies engaging T cells against CD20-positive lymphoma cells are under active investigation as both monotherapy and combination strategies [@Kaplon2019Antibodies; @Kaplon2020Antibodies; @Amini2022Preparing]. These developments, together with emerging insights from the tumor microenvironment and immune evasion mechanisms [@Vari2018Immune; @Nie2019Neutrophil], central nervous system prophylaxis strategies [@Schmitz2016CNS; @Grommes2019Comprehensive], and refined prognostic indices [@Ruppert2020International], have created a rapidly shifting therapeutic landscape that demands comprehensive synthesis.

The period from 2016 to 2026 thus represents a decade of unprecedented change in the frontline management of DLBCL. Molecular classification has matured from a research tool to a clinical decision-making framework. Novel immunotherapies have disrupted the therapeutic hierarchy. Yet significant challenges persist: high-risk molecular subtypes remain inadequately served, optimal sequencing of new agents is undefined, and the integration of molecular diagnostics into routine practice is incomplete. A systematic review of the current evidence is therefore timely and necessary. Furthermore, recent investigations into novel biomarkers, including PKC-delta expression [@Zhao2026Research] and nano-based pharmaceutical delivery systems [@Rodrguez2022NanoBased], suggest that the therapeutic landscape will continue to evolve rapidly, reinforcing the need for a comprehensive evidence synthesis to guide clinical practice and inform future trial design.

The rationale for this review stems from the critical gap between the rapidly transforming DLBCL treatment landscape and the lack of a unified synthesis integrating molecular subtyping, novel immunotherapies, and risk-adapted strategies across the past decade.

The objectives of this review are as follows:

1. To synthesize the current evidence on frontline chemoimmunotherapy regimens for DLBCL, with emphasis on the transition from R-CHOP to pola-R-CHP and other novel combinations.
2. To evaluate the clinical impact of molecular subtyping---including cell-of-origin classification, double-hit and double-expresser biology, and genomic classifiers---on treatment selection and prognostication.
3. To assess the emerging role of antibody--drug conjugates, bispecific antibodies, and other targeted agents in the frontline setting.
4. To review the evidence supporting earlier integration of CAR-T cell therapy for high-risk patients at initial diagnosis.
5. To critically appraise prognostic tools and risk stratification frameworks that inform treatment intensity and therapeutic strategy.
6. To identify unresolved questions and future research priorities that will guide the next generation of frontline DLBCL trials.
